top10.name: Bali
top10.description: Bali is Indonesia's most famous island destination, known for its stunning beaches, terraced rice paddies, and vibrant Hindu culture. It attracts millions of visitors annually for its world-class resorts, water sports, temples, and nightlife.

top10.name: Jakarta
top10.description: Jakarta is Indonesia's capital and largest city, serving as the main entry point for international visitors and the country's economic hub. Tourists visit for shopping, dining, museums, and business purposes.

top10.name: Yogyakarta
top10.description: The cultural heart of Java and Indonesia's artistic center, home to traditional batik, puppet shows, and numerous museums and galleries. It serves as the essential base for exploring Central Java's temples, with excellent local food, vibrant street life, and a thriving arts scene.

top10.name: Borobudur Temple
top10.description: The world's largest Buddhist temple complex, built in the 8th century with over 500 Buddha statues and intricate stone carvings spread across nine levels. It is one of Indonesia's most iconic monuments and a UNESCO World Heritage Site offering profound spiritual and architectural experiences.

top10.name: Lombok Island
top10.description: Lombok is the second-largest island in the Nusa Tenggara archipelago, offering pristine beaches, dramatic volcanic landscapes, and a more laid-back atmosphere than Bali. It's the essential hub for exploring the region with excellent beaches like Kuta and Senggigi, plus access to Mount Rinjani and the Gili Islands.

top10.name: Surabaya
top10.description: East Java's largest city and a major port, Surabaya is a vibrant urban center with a rich revolutionary history and growing modern attractions. It serves as the gateway to the region and offers museums, colonial architecture, and excellent local cuisine.

top10.name: Mount Bromo
top10.description: One of Indonesia's most iconic active volcanoes, Bromo offers dramatic moonlit sunrise hikes across the Sea of Sand with otherworldly crater views. It's the region's most popular natural attraction and a bucket-list experience for adventure travelers.

top10.name: Prambanan Temple
top10.description: A magnificent 10th-century Hindu temple with three soaring pointed towers rising dramatically from the landscape, representing the trinity of Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva. Its distinctive silhouette and intricate reliefs make it a UNESCO World Heritage Site and one of Java's most visually striking monuments.

top10.name: Bandung
top10.description: West Java's capital and largest city, known for its cool mountain climate, vibrant arts scene, and colonial architecture mixed with modern cafes and fashion outlets. It's a cultural hub offering museums, galleries, tea plantations, and serves as a base for exploring surrounding natural attractions.

top10.name: Lake Toba
top10.description: The world's largest volcanic lake and a UNESCO Global Geopark, offering stunning mountain scenery, cool highland climate, and opportunities for hiking and water activities. The serene landscape and Batak cultural villages make it North Sumatra's most iconic destination.

guide/festivals.json.title: Festivals & events
guide/festivals.json.subtitle: 42 celebrations through the year — pick a day to see what's on.

festivals.name: New Year's Day
festivals.description: Indonesia's official national celebration marking the start of the calendar year with public holidays and festivities across all regions.

festivals.name: Isra and Mi'raj
festivals.description: Islamic holiday commemorating Prophet Muhammad's night journey and ascension to heaven, observed with prayers and spiritual gatherings.

festivals.name: Chinese New Year
festivals.description: Celebrated by Indonesia's Chinese communities with vibrant parades, dragon dances, and family gatherings, particularly lively in Jakarta and Medan.

festivals.name: Nyepi (Balinese New Year)
festivals.description: The Balinese Hindu New Year marked by the Day of Silence where the entire island shuts down for introspection and spiritual cleansing, preceded by exuberant Ogoh-Ogoh parade.

festivals.name: Ramadan
festivals.description: The Islamic holy month of fasting observed throughout Indonesia with special prayers, family gatherings, and vibrant night markets in every city.

festivals.name: Eid al-Fitr (Lebaran)
festivals.description: Indonesia's biggest celebration marking the end of Ramadan with massive holiday exodus, family reunions, and feasting that dominates national life.

festivals.name: Makepung Jembrana Buffalo Race
festivals.description: Traditional water buffalo racing in Bali's Jembrana Regency where teams compete with decorated buffalo pulling wooden carts across rice paddies.

festivals.name: Vesakha Buddha Day
festivals.description: National holiday honoring Buddha's birth, enlightenment, and death with Buddhist ceremonies, temple pilgrimages, and candlelit processions.

festivals.name: Flores Komodo Festival
festivals.description: Celebrates the unique Komodo dragon and Flores culture with ceremonies, traditional dances, and showcases of local craftsmanship in this remote island region.

festivals.name: Eid al-Adha (Lebaran Haji)
festivals.description: Islamic festival of sacrifice commemorating Abraham's willingness to sacrifice his son, marked by animal sacrifices and distribution of meat to the poor.

festivals.name: Tumpak Landhep (Balinese Harvest Festival)
festivals.description: Ancient Balinese harvest thanksgiving festival featuring temple ceremonies, offerings, and traditional rituals celebrating the agricultural cycle.

festivals.name: Islamic New Year
festivals.description: The Islamic calendar's new year observed with special prayers and religious gatherings across Muslim-majority Indonesia.

festivals.name: Mawlid Nabi Muhammad
festivals.description: Birthday of Prophet Muhammad celebrated nationwide with Quranic recitations, sermon gatherings, and special prayers in mosques.

festivals.name: Garuda Wisnu Kencana Festival
festivals.description: Cultural festival at the iconic GWK statue featuring traditional dance performances, kite flying, and Balinese handicraft exhibitions.

festivals.name: Sade Village Festival
festivals.description: Cultural celebration in the traditional Sasak village showcasing unique indigenous traditions, weaving crafts, and horse racing.

festivals.name: Independence Day
festivals.description: Indonesia's most important national holiday commemorating independence from Dutch colonial rule with flag ceremonies, parades, and competitions nationwide.

festivals.name: Jakarta Fashion Week
festivals.description: Major fashion event showcasing Indonesian and international designers with runway shows and exhibitions in Southeast Asia's fashion capital.

festivals.name: Bali Arts Festival
festivals.description: Month-long celebration of Balinese arts featuring traditional dance, music, theater, painting, and sculpture performances at venues across Bali.

festivals.name: Rajah Fishing Festival
festivals.description: Traditional fishing competition in Balikpapan celebrating maritime heritage with boat races and traditional fishing demonstrations.

festivals.name: Manado Fiesta
festivals.description: Manado's main cultural festival featuring street parades, traditional music and dance performances, and exhibitions celebrating Minahasan culture.

festivals.name: Bathik Day
festivals.description: National celebration of Indonesia's UNESCO-recognized batik tradition with exhibitions, fashion shows, and workshops showcasing this iconic textile art.

festivals.name: Dayak Harvest Festival (Gawai Dayak)
festivals.description: Traditional harvest celebration of Dayak people featuring ritual ceremonies, traditional dance, and communal feasting in longhouses.
festivals.location: West Kalimantan and Sarawak border regions

festivals.name: Pushaka Festival
festivals.description: Palembang's cultural festival celebrating the city's heritage with traditional sports, music performances, and historical exhibitions.

festivals.name: Hang Nadim Festival
festivals.description: Batam's maritime festival honoring the legendary Hang Nadim with boat races, sea festivals, and cultural performances.

festivals.name: Mentawai Island Festival
festivals.description: Celebration of Mentawai indigenous culture featuring traditional tattoo exhibitions, tribal ceremonies, and surfing events on the island's world-famous waves.

festivals.name: Jember Fashion Carnival
festivals.description: Flamboyant street parade featuring elaborate costumes and creative float designs celebrating fashion, culture, and creativity in East Java.

festivals.name: Karimun Jawa Carnival
festivals.description: Island festival showcasing marine and maritime culture with boat races, traditional ceremonies, and seafood celebrations.

festivals.name: Borobudur Sunrise Festival
festivals.description: Spiritual celebration at the ancient Buddhist temple featuring meditation gatherings, traditional performances, and cultural activities at sunrise.

festivals.name: Ramayana Ballet at Prambanan
festivals.description: Open-air theatrical performance of the Hindu epic Ramayana against the backdrop of the illuminated 10th-century Prambanan temple.

festivals.name: Haul Syech Yusuf
festivals.description: Makassar's commemorative festival honoring Syech Yusuf with religious gatherings, Quranic recitations, and traditional ceremonies.

festivals.name: Kasepuhan Cultural Festival
festivals.description: Celebration of Sundanese indigenous culture and traditional leadership systems with ceremonies, traditional dress, and communal rituals.

festivals.name: Puncak Pass Festival
festivals.description: Mountain cultural festival featuring traditional Sundanese arts, crafts, and cuisine in the cool highlands near Jakarta.

festivals.name: Wonderful Indonesia Festival
festivals.description: National festival celebrating Indonesia's diverse cultures, cuisines, and traditions with performances and exhibitions from across the archipelago.

festivals.name: Labuan Bajo Jazz Festival
festivals.description: International jazz festival in the scenic port town near Komodo National Park featuring performances by regional and international jazz musicians.

festivals.name: Yogyakarta Craft Festival
festivals.description: Celebration of traditional Javanese batik, woodcarving, and silversmithing crafts with artisan exhibitions, workshops, and sales.

festivals.name: Banyuwangi Ethno Carnival
festivals.description: East Java's major cultural parade featuring elaborate floats, traditional costumes, and performances celebrating the region's ethnic diversity.

festivals.name: Halmahera Festival
festivals.description: Celebration of Maluku spice islands heritage with traditional ceremonies, spice exhibitions, and demonstrations of historic maritime trade.

festivals.name: Toraja Funeral Festival (Ma'nene)
festivals.description: Unique ancient ritual where deceased ancestors are exhumed, dressed, and paraded through villages in colorful ceremonies celebrating the cycle of life.

festivals.name: Banjarmasin Floating Market Festival
festivals.description: Celebration of Banjarmasin's iconic floating markets with traditional boat races, waterfront performances, and showcases of local produce.

festivals.name: Lampung Way Kambas Festival
festivals.description: Wildlife festival celebrating endangered Sumatran elephants with environmental awareness activities, animal demonstrations, and conservation exhibits.

festivals.name: Aceh Culture Festival
festivals.description: Celebration of Acehnese heritage featuring traditional war dances, poetry recitations, and exhibitions of Islamic arts and crafts.

festivals.name: Christmas Day
festivals.description: National public holiday celebrated nationwide with religious services, family gatherings, and festive decorations in churches and Christian communities.

guide/history.json.title: A Short History of Indonesia
guide/history.json.subtitle: From its beginnings to today — 13 eras that shaped it.

items.label: Early Settlement
items.summary: The first humans arrive and spread across the archipelago, developing distinct cultures and languages.
items.text: Archaeological evidence suggests that modern humans reached the Indonesian archipelago around 50,000 years ago, migrating from mainland Asia and adapting to island life. These early inhabitants were hunter-gatherers and seafarers who gradually populated islands from Sumatra to Papua. Over thousands of years, they developed sophisticated knowledge of agriculture, animal husbandry, and maritime navigation. The extreme biodiversity of the region shaped separate populations with unique customs, languages, and technologies. By 3000 BC, agricultural societies were firmly established across much of the archipelago.

items.label: Austronesian Expansion
items.summary: Seafaring Austronesian peoples spread their language, culture, and agricultural practices across the islands.
items.text: Beginning around 3000 BC, a new wave of Austronesian-speaking peoples migrated from Taiwan and Southeast Asia, gradually mixing with existing populations. These skilled navigators and farmers brought wet-rice cultivation, advanced canoe technology, and a common linguistic root that would eventually spawn hundreds of Indonesian languages. They established trade networks and settled in organized communities, from the coasts to interior highlands. Bronze and iron tools gradually replaced stone implements, enabling more efficient farming and craft production. This period laid the cultural and linguistic foundations that would define Indonesia for millennia.

items.label: Early Hindu-Buddhist Influence
items.summary: Indian merchants and scholars bring Hinduism and Buddhism, which blend with local beliefs to create unique syncretic kingdoms.
items.text: From around the 1st century AD, Indian traders and Brahmin priests arrived in the archipelago, bringing Hindu and Buddhist ideas that gradually took root among coastal and elite communities. Rather than replacing local animism, these religions merged with existing beliefs and practices, creating a distinctly Indonesian form of Buddhism and Hinduism. Early kingdoms like the Taruma Negara in Java and Srivijaya emerged, adopting Sanskrit, Hindu-Buddhist cosmology, and Indian political structures while maintaining local authority. Temple construction and elaborate rituals became symbols of royal power. By the 7th century, several competing kingdoms had established themselves as regional powers.

items.label: Srivijaya Dominance
items.summary: A maritime Buddhist empire based in Sumatra controls trade across Southeast Asia for seven centuries.
items.text: Srivijaya emerged as a powerful thalassocracy centered in Palembang, Sumatra, dominating maritime trade routes between China and India. Rather than controlling territory through armies, Srivijaya exercised power through naval superiority and control of strategic ports, monopolizing the spice trade. The empire patronized Buddhism, attracting scholars from across Asia and becoming a major center of Buddhist learning; the Chinese monk Yijing famously studied there for years. Srivijaya's wealth came from controlling the Straits of Malacca and ports throughout Sumatra and the Malay Peninsula. The empire gradually declined due to internal conflict and the rise of the Hindu-Buddhist kingdom of Majapahit on Java.

items.label: Sailendra and Sanjaya Rivalry
items.summary: Two competing Hindu and Buddhist dynasties build monumental temples in central Java, creating a cultural golden age.
items.text: Central Java saw the rise of two powerful dynasties, the Sailendra (Buddhist) and Sanjaya (Hindu), who alternated control and occasionally ruled jointly over territories in the interior. The Sailendra family commissioned the construction of Borobudur, the world's largest Buddhist monument, around 800 AD, reflecting unprecedented wealth and organizational power. The Sanjaya dynasty responded with their own monumental temples, including the Hindu complex at Prambanan. These religious monuments embodied sophisticated engineering, artistic achievement, and deep theological knowledge, transforming Java into a center of Buddhist and Hindu civilization. The period ended with political fragmentation and the gradual shift of power toward East Java.

items.label: East Javanese Kingdoms
items.summary: Eastern Java becomes the archipelago's dominant power, producing the Hindu-Buddhist synthesis that defines Indonesian culture.
items.text: Following central Java's decline, a series of powerful kingdoms emerged in East Java, including the Kediri, Singhasari, and most importantly Majapahit. The Majapahit Empire, established in 1293 by Kertanegara and consolidated by Hayam Wuruk in the 14th century, grew into the most extensive kingdom yet seen in the region, controlling territories from Sumatra to Papua. Hayam Wuruk's reign (1331–1389) saw extraordinary artistic and literary achievements, including the composition of the Pax Majapahit philosophy that celebrated harmony among diverse kingdoms. Trade, temple construction, and administrative sophistication reached new heights under Majapahit's bureaucratic system. The empire's eventual decline in the early 16th century resulted from internal conflicts and the rise of Islamic sultanates along Java's coast.

items.label: Gradual Islamization
items.summary: Islam spreads through trade and migration, gradually converting the archipelago's coastal populations and eventual becoming dominant.
items.text: Beginning in the 13th century, Muslim merchants from India and the Middle East established trading posts and communities along the coasts of Sumatra, the Malay Peninsula, and Java. Rather than arriving through conquest initially, Islam spread through intermarriage, Sufi missionary work, and the obvious appeal of Islamic commercial networks. Port cities like Aceh, Bantam, and Makassar converted first and became centers for Islamic scholarship and sultanate authority. Coastal sultans adopted Islamic law and titles, gradually replacing Hindu-Buddhist kingdoms with Islamic states. By 1527, the Hindu-Buddhist Majapahit had finally fallen to Muslim sultanates, and Islam had become the dominant religion across most of the archipelago.

items.label: European Arrival
items.summary: Portuguese and Dutch traders arrive seeking spices, introducing European competition and eventually colonization.
items.text: In 1512, Portuguese ships under Afonso de Albuquerque reached Maluku, seeking control of the lucrative clove and nutmeg trade. The Portuguese established forts and trading posts but lacked the resources to conquer the vast archipelago, controlling only scattered ports. In the early 1600s, the Dutch East India Company (VOC) arrived with superior naval power and aggressive trading practices, gradually displacing the Portuguese and establishing monopolies over key spice-producing regions. The VOC made alliances with certain sultans, played rivals against each other, and used military force to maintain their trading monopoly. By 1700, the Dutch had become the dominant European power, though they controlled only fragments of the archipelago and faced constant resistance.

items.label: Dutch Colonial Expansion
items.summary: The Dutch East India Company gradually expands its territorial control, transforming spice trading posts into a full colonial empire.
items.text: Over the 18th and 19th centuries, the VOC and later the Dutch colonial government methodically conquered and annexed territories across the archipelago, extending their control from coastal trading posts into the interior. Major wars of conquest included the long Java War (1825–1830) against the Islamic resistance leader Diponegoro, which cost thousands of lives but ultimately secured Dutch control of Java. The Dutch introduced new agricultural systems, especially large-scale plantation farming of coffee, sugar, and rubber, generating enormous wealth for the colonial government and Dutch companies while exploiting Indonesian laborers. Colonial authority was exercised through appointed officials and cooperating local rulers, creating a hierarchical bureaucracy. By 1900, the Dutch controlled virtually the entire archipelago, transforming it into the Dutch East Indies, one of the world's most valuable colonies.

items.label: Nationalist Awakening
items.summary: Indonesian intellectuals and activists organized a powerful independence movement that ultimately defeated Dutch colonialism.
items.text: In the early 20th century, Dutch education and modernization inadvertently created an Indonesian educated class that began questioning colonial rule and developing nationalist ideas. Organizations like Budi Utomo (1908) and the Indonesian National Party (1927) emerged, with leaders like Sukarno and Hatta advocating for independence through political action and protest. The Dutch responded with repression, imprisoning nationalist leaders throughout the 1930s, but this only strengthened resistance sentiment. When Japan invaded during World War II, the Dutch colonial government collapsed, and nationalist leaders were released and briefly cooperated with the Japanese occupiers. On August 17, 1945, immediately after Japan's defeat, Sukarno and Hatta proclaimed Indonesian independence, launching a four-year armed struggle against the Dutch.

items.label: Independence and Early Republic
items.summary: Indonesia achieves independence after a violent struggle, then endures political instability, communal violence, and authoritarian rule.
items.text: The Indonesian War of Independence (1945–1949) saw fierce fighting between nationalist forces and the Dutch, ending with international pressure forcing the Dutch to recognize Indonesian sovereignty. The new nation was deeply fractured, with competing political parties, Islamic versus secular visions, ethnic tensions, and regional rebellion threats. President Sukarno navigated these conflicts through charismatic leadership and a policy of balancing communist, Islamic, and military forces, but instability deepened as communism grew. In 1965, a failed coup allegedly involving communists triggered a massive, violent crackdown that killed between 500,000 and over a million people. General Suharto rose to power, consolidated authoritarian control, and by 1967 had forced Sukarno from office, establishing the New Order regime.

items.label: Suharto's New Order
items.summary: An authoritarian military regime achieves rapid economic growth but rules through oppression, corruption, and regional exploitation.
items.text: General Suharto's New Order regime brought relative stability and rapid industrialization, transforming Indonesia into a middle-income country through oil revenues and manufacturing. However, the government maintained strict control through military dominance, suppression of dissent, curtailment of press freedom, and imprisonment of political opponents. Massive corruption enriched Suharto and his associates while regional resources were extracted and exploited, especially in East Timor, Aceh, and Papua. The regime violently suppressed independence movements and ethnic conflicts, committing serious human rights abuses that went largely unpunished. Growing economic inequality, resentment over crony capitalism, and desire for democratic reform built pressure throughout the 1980s and 1990s.

items.label: Reformasi and Democratic Transition
items.summary: The Asian financial crisis triggers mass protests that topple Suharto, followed by democratization, decentralization, and gradual institutional reform.
items.text: The 1997 Asian financial crisis devastated Indonesia's economy, sparking widespread unemployment and poverty that ignited massive protests against Suharto's regime in 1998. Students, workers, and intellectuals demanded democracy and accountability, and facing impossible odds, Suharto resigned in May 1998 after 32 years in power. Indonesia embarked on reformasi (reform), adopting a new democratic constitution, holding competitive elections (first in 1999), and significantly decentralizing power to provinces and districts. The transition has been messy but genuine, with independent media emerging, civil society expanding, and power alternating between political parties. Challenges remain including regional conflicts (resolved in East Timor, ongoing in Papua), communal violence, corruption, and inequality, but Indonesia has become the world's largest Muslim-majority democracy with a vibrant pluralistic society.

guide/food.json.title: Food & Drink
guide/food.json.subtitle: How to eat well in Indonesia — customs, dishes and where to go.

items.label: Kopi Tubruk
items.text: Ground coffee grounds settle at the bottom of your cup—wait a moment before sipping so you don't get a mouthful of sediment. It's strong, unfiltered, and often served with sweetened condensed milk (kopi susu) or black (kopi hitam).

items.label: Jamu and Coffee Blends
items.text: Many Indonesian coffee shops blend their kopi with turmeric, ginger, or other traditional herbal remedies (jamu). Ask what's in it before ordering if you have preferences or allergies.

items.label: Warung Kopi vs. Café
items.text: Cheap street-level warungs serve authentic kopi tubruk for under 10,000 IDR; trendy cafés in Jakarta and Bali charge 40,000+ IDR for specialty roasts. Both are worth visiting for different reasons.

items.label: Sugar is Standard
items.text: Indonesian coffee is traditionally very sweet. If you want it less sweet or black, say 'sedikit gula' (little sugar) or 'tanpa gula' (no sugar) when ordering.

items.label: Rendang
items.text: Meat (usually beef) slow-cooked in coconut milk and spice paste until rich and concentrated. This is Indonesia's most famous dish—try it in Padang restaurants for authentic versions, not watered-down tourist versions.

items.label: Soto Ayam
items.text: Turmeric-based chicken soup with rice, typically served with a hard-boiled egg and potato. Order it for breakfast or lunch; it's warming, affordable, and available everywhere from street stalls to restaurants.

items.label: Gado-Gado
items.text: Blanched vegetables (cabbage, bean sprouts, green beans) topped with peanut sauce, fried tofu, and a hard-boiled egg. It's vegetarian-friendly and a reliable lunch option; sauce quality varies wildly so eat at busy stalls with high turnover.

items.label: Nasi Goreng
items.text: Fried rice cooked with soy sauce, garlic, chili, and usually egg and protein. Every cook makes it differently; it's simple comfort food but tourist restaurants often charge 5× the price of warung versions.

items.label: Bakso
items.text: Beef meatball soup served in a savory broth with noodles, vegetables, and fried shallots. It's cheap, hearty, and found at dedicated bakso stalls; ask for 'bakso daging' (beef) to avoid organ-meat versions.

items.label: Satay [Sate]
items.text: Skewered meat grilled and served with peanut sauce (often with a squeeze of lime and sliced shallots). Chicken, beef, and goat are common; the meat quality and sauce make all the difference—stick to busy stalls.

items.label: Point and Ask
items.text: Many warungs have no English menus—point at what other diners are eating or ask 'Apa yang bagus?' (What's good?). Locals are usually happy to recommend the day's best dish.

items.label: Spice Levels
items.text: Indonesian food is often spicy; say 'tidak pedas' (not spicy) or 'sedikit pedas' (a little spicy) when ordering. They will respect this—don't be shy about asking.

items.label: Restaurant vs. Warung Pricing
items.text: Restaurants with printed menus and table service will charge 2–5× more than street-level warungs for similar food. Neither is 'better,' but understand the price difference reflects service and ambiance, not quality.

items.label: Rice is a Staple, Not Automatic
items.text: Many dishes come with rice included, but confirm by asking 'dengan nasi?' (with rice?). Some noodle dishes already include carbs, so you may not want extra.

items.label: Avoid Uncooked Vegetables in Street Food
items.text: Raw vegetables in warung salads and fresh spring rolls can carry water-borne parasites if not handled properly. Order cooked options or eat at busier, cleaner-looking stalls with visible food preparation.

items.label: Warung Kopi
items.text: Humble coffee shops open early (5–6am) serving kopi, eggs, toast, and simple noodles. These are where locals eat breakfast and are cheap, authentic, and reliable for genuine Indonesian food.

items.label: Pasar Malam (Night Markets)
items.text: Temporary markets set up in the evening selling street food from dozens of stalls. Prices are rock-bottom, variety is high, and they're socially lively—go with a local or arrive early while stalls are fresh.

items.label: Rumah Makan (Family Restaurants)
items.text: Mid-range sit-down restaurants serving regional Indonesian cuisine. They're slightly pricier than warungs but offer consistent quality, English menus, and a good introduction to regional dishes.

items.label: Food Courts in Malls
items.text: Modern shopping centers have food courts with multiple Indonesian and Asian vendors, clean facilities, and prices between warungs and restaurants. Useful when you want choice and hygiene certainty.

items.label: Tourist Restaurant Traps
items.text: Restaurants in Seminyak (Bali), central Jakarta, and other tourist areas drastically inflate prices and reduce authenticity. Eat where locals eat—one block away from main tourist streets, prices often drop 50%.

items.label: Teh Tarik (Pulled Tea)
items.text: Strong black tea mixed with condensed milk, traditionally poured from one cup to another to create a frothy top. It's sweet, creamy, and iconic—order at any warung kopi or Malaysian-Indonesian café.

items.label: Jus Buah (Fresh Fruit Juice)
items.text: Pressed juice from local fruits (mangoes, papayas, watermelon, passion fruit) blended with ice and sometimes condensed milk. Cheap and refreshing; order at juice stalls but ask if they use filtered water to be safe.

items.label: Ais Campur (Mixed Ice)
items.text: A dessert-drink combining shaved ice, condensed milk, brown sugar syrup, and tropical fruits or beans. It's sweet and cooling—popular as an afternoon treat in hot weather.

items.label: Beer and Alcohol Availability
items.text: Alcohol is widely available in urban areas and tourist regions but less so in conservative Muslim neighborhoods. Bintang and Anker are the main local beers; spirits are expensive due to taxes.

items.label: Tap Water and Ice Safety
items.text: Tap water is not safe to drink; order bottled water (air mineral) or drinks made with boiled water. Ice at tourist restaurants is typically fine; street stall ice can be risky.

items.label: Tuak (Traditional Rice Wine)
items.text: A fermented alcoholic drink from palm sap or rice, particularly popular in Sumatra and parts of Bali. It's low alcohol, sweet, and often homemade—sample it at local celebrations or dedicated shops, but quality and hygiene vary.

items.label: Right Hand for Eating and Passing
items.text: In Indonesian culture, the left hand is traditionally considered unclean. Use your right hand to eat, pass dishes, and hand over money, even if you're left-handed—it's a sign of respect.

items.label: Communal Dining
items.text: Indonesian meals are often shared family-style with multiple dishes in the center. Take small portions and refill your plate rather than heaping everything on at once.

items.label: Tipping is Not Obligatory
items.text: Tipping is not customary in warungs or street stalls. Upscale restaurants may include a 10% service charge; if not, a small tip (5–10%) is appreciated but never expected.

items.label: Remove Shoes When Entering Homes
items.text: If invited to dine at someone's home, remove your shoes at the entrance. Wait to be shown where to sit and don't start eating until the host begins.

items.label: Compliment the Food
items.text: Indonesians take pride in their cooking. Saying 'Enak!' (Delicious!) or 'Lezat!' (Tasty!) is always appreciated and costs nothing.

items.label: Finishing Your Plate
items.text: Leaving food on your plate can be seen as wasteful or disrespectful, especially in poorer areas. Take only what you can eat; it's acceptable to ask for a smaller portion.

guide/transport.json.title: Getting Around
guide/transport.json.subtitle: Trains, roads, taxis and how to move around Indonesia.

items.label: Book with PT Kereta Api
items.text: Use the official KAI website or mobile app to book long-distance trains across Java and Sumatra. Tickets are cheaper online and you avoid queues at stations.

items.label: Arrive early for departures
items.text: Trains in Indonesia often depart on time, so arrive at least 30 minutes before your scheduled departure. Platforms can be crowded during peak hours.
Pickpocketing is common on busy trains, especially overnight services. Keep valuables in a money belt and be cautious during boarding and alighting.

items.label: Seat reservations required
items.text: All long-distance trains require seat reservations; you cannot just buy a platform ticket. Reserve ahead as popular routes fill quickly.

items.label: Night trains offer good value
items.text: Overnight sleeper trains between Jakarta and Yogyakarta or Surabaya are comfortable and save on accommodation costs.

items.label: Use TransJakarta in the capital
items.text: Jakarta's bus rapid transit (BRT) system is fast, air-conditioned, and affordable. Buy a card at stations and tap on entry and exit.

items.label: City buses are very cheap
items.text: Local buses (angkot/minibus) cost Rp3,500–5,000 per ride. Tell the conductor your destination and pay when you get off.
Buses are often overcrowded during rush hours and pickpocketing occurs. Keep bags in front and avoid loose valuables.

items.label: Download Moovit or Google Maps
items.text: These apps show bus routes, real-time arrivals, and transfers in major cities like Jakarta, Bandung, and Surabaya.

items.label: Use Grab and Gojek apps
items.text: These ride-hailing apps are ubiquitous, safer than hailing taxis on the street, and show fares upfront. Both offer motorbike (ojek) and car options.
Avoid unlicensed taxis and always use metered cabs or app-based services. Non-metered taxis often overcharge tourists significantly.

items.label: Ride-sharing is cheaper than taxis
items.text: Grab and Gojek rides cost 20–40% less than traditional metered taxis in cities like Jakarta and Bali.

items.label: Motorbike taxis require helmets
items.text: Wearing a helmet is mandatory by law on Grab Bike and Gojek motorbike rides. Fines apply if caught without one.

items.label: Pay via app to avoid scams
items.text: Always pay through the Grab or Gojek app rather than cash to prevent disputes or overcharging at the end of your ride.
Traffic in Jakarta and major cities is chaotic and driving rules are often ignored. Consider hiring a driver instead of self-driving.

items.label: International driving permit required
items.text: Carry an International Driving Permit (IDP) plus your home license. Fines for driving without one can reach Rp500,000–2,000,000.

items.label: Hire a driver through rental agencies
items.text: Budget-friendly driver hire costs Rp300,000–500,000 per day and is safer than self-driving on unfamiliar roads.
Road conditions outside cities are poor and many roads lack street lighting at night. Avoid driving after dark in rural areas.

items.label: Toll roads on Java
items.text: Major highways (especially Jakarta–Bandung–Yogyakarta) use electronic toll cards. Buy a card at toll gates or convenience stores.

items.label: Soekarno-Hatta is Jakarta's main hub
items.text: This is the largest airport in Indonesia. Allow 2–3 hours for domestic flights and 3–4 hours for international flights.

items.label: Use airport taxis or ride apps
items.text: Book a Grab or use the airport taxi counter for fixed fares. Avoid touts outside offering rides at inflated prices.
Luggage storage facilities are limited at most airports. Plan ahead if you have long layovers or multiple bags.

items.label: Arrive early for domestic flights
items.text: Check in at least 2 hours before domestic departures and 3 hours before international flights. Airlines enforce these strictly.

items.label: Major carriers include Garuda, Lion Air, and Batik Air
items.text: Book in advance online for better fares. Garuda is premium; Lion Air and Batik Air are budget-friendly but sometimes experience delays.

items.label: Inter-island ferries connect major islands
items.text: Pelni operates large overnight ferries linking Java, Bali, Sulawesi, and beyond. Book via their website or travel agents.

items.label: Speedboats for short island hops
items.text: Fast ferries connect Bali–Lombok, Bali–Gili Islands, and other nearby islands. Book through operators like Gili Cat or Scoot Fast.
Ferry schedules are weather-dependent; services may be cancelled during heavy rains or strong seas. Confirm departures the day before.
Safety standards on some private speedboats are poor and life jackets may be unavailable. Choose established operators with good reviews.

items.label: Overnight ferries offer budget accommodation
items.text: Sleeping aboard a Pelni ferry costs less than a hotel and includes meals, making long journeys economical.

items.label: Buy TransJakarta cards in advance
items.text: Get a rechargeable Jaklingko card at stations and convenience stores for seamless bus and ferry transfers across Jakarta.

items.label: Book trains and flights online
items.text: Use official websites (KAI for trains, airline sites) or trusted apps like Traveloka and Tiket.com to avoid counterfeits and scams.
Counterfeit tickets are sold near train and bus stations. Only buy from official counters, authorized agents, or verified online platforms.

items.label: Tourist passes exist in some cities
items.text: Yogyakarta and some other cities offer multi-day tourist passes covering public transport and attractions—ask at the tourist information center.

items.label: Keep proof of purchase
items.text: Always carry your ticket confirmation (digital or printed) as proof of booking for trains and planes.

items.label: Buy local SIM cards on arrival
items.text: Major carriers include Telkomsel, Indosat, and XL Axiata. SIM cards cost Rp5,000–10,000 and are sold at airports, convenience stores, and mobile shops.

items.label: Prepaid plans are widely available
items.text: Buy data packages (5GB for Rp50,000–100,000) that include calls and texts. Top-ups can be done at any convenience store or via the carrier's app.

items.label: Telkomsel has the best coverage
items.text: This is the largest carrier and has the most reliable signal, especially outside cities and on remote islands.
Mobile data speeds are sometimes slow and coverage gaps exist in rural areas. Download offline maps and important information before traveling.

items.label: Airport WiFi and hotspots exist
items.text: Most hotels, restaurants, and malls offer free WiFi, but speeds vary. A local SIM is more reliable for getting around.
